intro:
One Hebrew word, rûaḥ (Greek pneuma), carries three meanings that the Bible holds together as one image: wind, breath, and Spirit. The same word names the gale that bends the trees, the breath in a living creature's lungs, and the Spirit of God himself. This overlap is not a confusion of language but a revelation: the unseen, sovereign, life-giving power that moves through the world is the breath of the living God.

The motif opens at creation, where "the Spirit of God" (rûaḥ) hovers over the face of the waters (Genesis 1:2), and humanity comes alive when God breathes into the dust "the breath of life" (Genesis 2:7). All life is borrowed breath: "when you take away their breath, they die… when you send forth your Spirit, they are created" (Psalm 104:29–30). The same wind serves God's saving acts — an east wind drives back the sea so Israel passes through (Exodus 14:21) — and his judgments, for "by the breath of his mouth" the wicked perish (Job 4:9). The wind is invisible, untameable, and entirely at God's command (Psalm 135:7).

The prophets see that what creation needs is a fresh outpouring of this breath. In Ezekiel's vision of the valley of dry bones, the word is sounded three ways at once: the prophet summons the rûaḥ — "Come from the four winds, O breath, and breathe on these slain, that they may live" — and God explains, "I will put my Spirit within you, and you shall live" (Ezekiel 37:9; Ezekiel 37:14). Resurrection and the gift of the Spirit are the same act of divine breath. Joel foretells the day when God will pour out his Spirit on all flesh (Joel 2:28).

In the Gospels Jesus draws on the full force of the image. To Nicodemus he says, "The wind blows where it wishes… so it is with everyone who is born of the Spirit" (John 3:8) — the new birth is as sovereign and mysterious as the wind. On the evening of his resurrection the new Adam reverses Eden's loss: he breathes on his disciples and says, "Receive the Holy Spirit" (John 20:22). At Pentecost the promise is poured out publicly: a sound "like a mighty rushing wind" fills the house, and the Spirit comes upon the church (Acts 2:2–4).

The image matters because it tells us how God gives life — not from a distance, but by imparting his own breath. The Spirit who hovered over the first creation, who breathes life into dust and raises the dead, is the same Spirit poured out through the risen Christ to make a new creation. To be a Christian is to live by a borrowed breath that has become a permanent indwelling: the very rûaḥ of God, given without measure in his Son.
